content.plain_english: This week in Year 2 maths, your child is working on Shape. The learning focuses on: identify and describe the properties of 2d shapes, including the number of sides and line symmetry in a vertical line; identify and describe the properties of 3d shapes, including the number of edges, vertices and faces; and identify 2d shapes on the surface of 3d shapes. These skills build directly on what they have already mastered and prepare them for the next steps in the programme of study.
content.in_school: Children handle, sort, and classify 2-D and 3-D shapes, drawing and describing their properties. They use protractors, mirrors, and squared paper to explore angles, symmetry, and position. Explaining what they notice in precise mathematical language is a key part of every lesson.
content.home_activity: Go on a shape hunt around the house. How many rectangles can you spot? Any cylinders in the kitchen? Count vertices and edges on a cereal box or tin. For angles, look for right angles (corners of doors) and ask whether other angles are acute or obtuse.
dinner_table_questions: Can you show me how to identify and describe the properties of 2d shapes, including the number of sides and line symmetry in a vertical line?
Was it easy or tricky to identify and describe the properties of 3d shapes, including the number of edges, vertices and faces? Why?
How would you explain today's maths to someone who has never seen it before?

key_vocabulary.definition: A flat shape with length and width but no thickness — e.g. triangle, circle.
A solid shape with length, width, and height — e.g. cube, sphere, cone.
When one half of a shape is a mirror image of the other.
An angle of exactly 90° — the same as the corner of a square.
Lines that meet at a right angle (90°).

example_questions: Name a quadrilateral with all sides equal.
Draw a shape with exactly 1 line of symmetry.
How many right angles does a rectangle have?
content.times_table_tip: Year 2 children learn their 2, 5, 10, and 3 times tables. The 3 times table is the new one this year. Try chanting together: 3, 6, 9, 12, 15 … Clapping a beat as you say the numbers helps the rhythm stick.
